title: DE4W Capstone Project
text: 1 Welcome

title: DE4W Capstone Project
section: 1.1 Building a Live Activity Recognition System
text:
1.1 Building a Live Activity Recognition System
Welcome to the DE4W Capstone Project.
During this semester you learned many individual concepts:

wearable sensors
data collection
preprocessing
feature engineering
machine learning
evaluation
deployment

Today, we combine all of these concepts into one complete engineering project.

title: DE4W Capstone Project
text:
1.2 What Will We Build?
By the end of this project, you will have built a complete activity recognition system capable of recognizing human activities in real time using smartphone accelerometer data.

title: DE4W Capstone Project
text:
1.3 Project Pipeline





flowchart LR

A[Smartphone]
-->B[Accelerometer]

B-->C[Windowing]

C-->D[Feature Engineering]

D-->E[Random Forest]

E-->F[FastAPI]

F-->G[Streamlit]

G-->H[Live Prediction]

title: DE4W Capstone Project
text:
1.4 Learning Outcomes
After completing this project you will be able to

build an end-to-end machine learning pipeline
engineer features from wearable sensor data
train and evaluate a machine learning model
deploy a trained model as a REST API
create an interactive dashboard
perform live activity recognition using your own smartphone

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.1 Today We Build a System
Most machine learning tutorials end after model training.
This project goes further.
We will build a complete system:
flowchart TD

A[Raw Sensor Data]
-->B[Windowing]

B-->C[Feature Engineering]

C-->D[Machine Learning]

D-->E[Evaluation]

E-->F[Model Export]

F-->G[FastAPI]

G-->H[Streamlit Dashboard]

H-->I[Live Prediction]

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.2 Why This Matters
A trained model is not yet an application.
A real wearable machine learning system also needs:

reliable data handling
reproducible preprocessing
consistent feature extraction
realistic evaluation
model export
an API
a user interface
live sensor integration







TipEngineering Insight



In real-world machine learning projects, the model is only one component of a larger system.

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.3 The Scenario
Imagine you joined a small digital health startup.
Your task:

Build a system that recognizes whether a person is walking, sitting or standing using only smartphone accelerometer data.

The system should eventually work live with a real smartphone.

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.4 Target Activities
We focus on three activities:



Activity
Why it is useful




Walking
Dynamic, periodic movement


Sitting
Low movement, common daily activity


Standing
Low movement, hard to distinguish from sitting



This simplified setup keeps the focus on the complete engineering pipeline.

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:

This project connects the entire course.



Course Topic
Where It Appears in the Project




Sensors
Smartphone accelerometer


Data Collection
WISDM and phyphox


Data Engineering
Loading, cleaning, structuring


Preprocessing
Magnitude and windowing


Feature Engineering
Statistical window features


Machine Learning
Random Forest


Evaluation
Random split vs subject-wise split


Deployment
FastAPI and Streamlit

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.6 Required Tools
You need:

Python
uv
Quarto
Jupyter kernel
phyphox app
WISDM phone accelerometer data

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.7 Networking Requirement
For the live demo, your laptop and smartphone must be able to communicate in the same local network.






WarningImportant



University networks such as eduroam may block direct communication between devices.
If phyphox is not reachable from your browser, use:

smartphone hotspot
local WiFi
travel router

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
section: 2.9 How to Work Through This Project
text:
2.9 How to Work Through This Project
Each major step follows the same pattern:

Understand the problem.
Implement the solution.
Inspect the output.
Discuss what could go wrong.
Continue to the next component.

The goal is not to copy code.
The goal is to understand the engineering decisions behind the code.

title: 2  Introduction and Motivation
text:
2.10 Chapter Summary
In this chapter you learned:

what we will build
why deployment matters
how the project connects to the semester
which tools are required
why networking matters for live sensor demos
how the project is structured

Next, we will inspect the WISDM dataset and understand the raw smartphone accelerometer signals.

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
text:
3.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
text:
understand the structure of the WISDM dataset
explain how wearable sensor data is organized
identify the most important attributes of the dataset
understand why exploratory data analysis (EDA) is the first step of every machine learning project

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
section: 3.2 Why Do We Start with the Data?
text:
3.2 Why Do We Start with the Data?
Many beginners believe that machine learning starts by choosing an algorithm.
In reality, every successful machine learning project starts with understanding the available data.
Before writing a single line of model training code, we should answer questions such as:

What information is available?
How was the data collected?
Which sensors were used?
What do the labels represent?
Are there missing values?
Are all activities equally represented?







TipEngineering Insight



Experienced data scientists usually spend much more time understanding and preparing data than training models.

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
text:
3.3 The WISDM Dataset
Throughout this project we use the WISDM Smartphone and Smartwatch Activity Dataset.
For simplicity, we only use smartphone accelerometer data.

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
text:
3.4 Raw File Structure
Each participant is stored in a separate file.
data_1600_accel_phone.txt

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
3  Understanding the Dataset
text:

subject_id
Participant identifier


activity_code

timestamp

Acceleration along x-axis


y
Acceleration along y-axis


z

title: 3  Understanding the Dataset
text:
3.7 Activity Mapping

ACTIVITY_MAP = {
    'A':'walking','B':'jogging','C':'stairs','D':'sitting','E':'standing',
    'F':'typing','G':'brushing_teeth','H':'eating_soup','I':'eating_chips',
    'J':'eating_pasta','K':'drinking','L':'eating_sandwich','M':'kicking',
    'O':'catch','P':'dribbling','Q':'writing','R':'clapping','S':'folding_clothes'
}

TARGET_ACTIVITIES=['walking','sitting','standing']







NoteRemember



We keep the complete activity mapping although we currently use only three activities.

crumbs: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data

title: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data
text:
4.2 Why This Chapter Matters
Before we create windows, extract features, or train a model, we need to make sure that we can load and understand the raw data.
This chapter turns a folder of raw text files into a structured table that we can analyze and process further.






TipEngineering Insight



A clean data loading step is one of the most important parts of a reproducible machine learning project.
If the loading logic is unclear or inconsistent, every later result becomes questionable.
crumbs: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data

title: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data
text:
4.3 Step 1 — Import Required Libraries

4.3.1 Goal
We import the libraries needed for data loading, numerical processing and visualization.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

title: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data
text:
4.4 Step 2 — Configure Project Paths

4.4.1 Goal
We define where the project root and the data directory are located.

current_path = Path.cwd().resolve()

for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents):
    if (parent / "app").exists():
        PROJECT_ROOT = parent
        break
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.")

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from app.config import DATA_DIR
from app.data_loader import (
    ACTIVITY_MAP,
    TARGET_ACTIVITIES,
    load_single_wisdm_file,
    load_wisdm_phone_accel,
)
from app.pipeline import add_magnitude


print("Project root:", PROJECT_ROOT)
print("Data directory:", DATA_DIR)

The raw WISDM files use activity codes such as A, D, and E.
We map these codes to readable names.

This makes it easier to extend the project later.

crumbs: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data

title: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data
text:
4.11 Step 9 — Plot One Activity

4.11.1 Goal
We visualize x, y, z and magnitude for one activity.

activity = "walking"

sample = (
    df[df["activity"] == activity]
    .head(400)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))
plt.plot(sample["x"], label="x")
plt.plot(sample["y"], label="y")
plt.plot(sample["z"], label="z")
plt.plot(sample["magnitude"], label="magnitude", linewidth=2)

plt.title(f"Phone accelerometer signal: {activity}")
plt.xlabel("Sample")
plt.ylabel("Acceleration [m/s²]")
plt.legend()
plt.show()










4.11.2 Discussion
Walking usually produces a more dynamic and periodic signal than sitting or standing.
crumbs: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data

title: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data
text:
4.12 Step 10 — Compare Activities

4.12.1 Goal
We compare the magnitude signal for walking, sitting and standing.

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

for activity in TARGET_ACTIVITIES:
    signal = (
        df[df["activity"] == activity]["magnitude"]
        .head(400)
        .reset_index(drop=True)
    )
    plt.plot(signal, label=activity)

plt.title("Magnitude comparison")
plt.xlabel("Sample")
plt.ylabel("Magnitude")
plt.legend()
plt.show()










4.12.2 Discussion
Walking should usually be easier to distinguish because it contains stronger and more regular movement.
Sitting and standing are more similar and may be harder to separate.
crumbs: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data

title: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data
text:
4.13 Step 11 — Estimate the Sampling Rate

4.13.1 Goal
The WISDM documentation states that the data was collected at approximately 20 Hz.
We estimate the sampling rate from timestamps.

subject = df["subject_id"].iloc[0]
activity = "walking"

sample = df[
    (df["subject_id"] == subject)
    & (df["activity"] == activity)
].sort_values("timestamp")

dt = sample["timestamp"].diff().dropna()

sampling_rate = 1e9 / dt.mean()

sampling_rate

np.float64(19.85939378331699)




4.13.2 Expected Output
The result should be close to 20 Hz.






NoteRemember



Documentation is important, but data engineers should still verify assumptions directly from the data.
crumbs: 4  Loading and Exploring the Data

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to

title: 5  Windowing
text:
explain why continuous sensor streams must be segmented into windows,
use reusable windowing code from the app/ package,
create windows for one activity,
create windows safely per subject and activity,
understand how window size affects live prediction.

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.2 Setup

from pathlib import Path
import sys

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

current_path = Path.cwd().resolve()

for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents):
    if (parent / "app").exists():
        PROJECT_ROOT = parent
        break
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.")

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from app.config import WINDOW_SIZE
from app.data_loader import load_wisdm_phone_accel
from app.pipeline import add_magnitude
from app.windowing import create_windows, create_grouped_windows

DATA_DIR = PROJECT_ROOT / "data"

df = load_wisdm_phone_accel(DATA_DIR)
df = add_magnitude(df)







ImportantSoftware Engineering Principle



This chapter does not redefine create_windows().
Instead, it imports reusable project code from app.windowing.
This follows the Don’t Repeat Yourself (DRY) principle.

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.3 Why Do We Need Windowing?
Wearable sensors produce a continuous stream of measurements.
Classical machine learning algorithms expect fixed-size samples.
Windowing bridges this gap by splitting a continuous stream into equally sized segments.

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.4 Step 1 — Create Windows for Walking

walking_df = (
    df[df["activity"] == "walking"]
    .sort_values("timestamp")
    .copy()
)

walking_windows = create_windows(
    walking_df,
    window_size=WINDOW_SIZE,
)

len(walking_windows)

2798

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.6 Step 3 — Visualize One Window

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

plt.plot(first_window["magnitude"])

plt.title("One Walking Window")
plt.xlabel("Sample")
plt.ylabel("Magnitude")
plt.show()

title: 5  Windowing
section: 5.7 Step 4 — Create Windows for the Entire Dataset
text:
5.7 Step 4 — Create Windows for the Entire Dataset

all_windows = create_grouped_windows(
    df,
    window_size=WINDOW_SIZE,
)

len(all_windows)

8066








WarningCommon Pitfall



Always create windows separately for each subject and activity.
Otherwise, a window may contain multiple activities.

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.8 Checkpoint

Why do we need windowing?
What does a 100-sample window represent at 20 Hz?
Why should windows not span multiple activities?
Why is the window size relevant for live prediction?

title: 5  Windowing
text:
5.9 Summary
In this chapter you learned

why windowing is necessary,
how reusable project code is imported,
how to create windows safely,
why window size controls the trade-off between stability and responsiveness.

In the next chapter we will extract numerical features from every window.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
explain why feature engineering is needed for classical machine learning,
convert sensor windows into numerical feature vectors,
use reusable feature extraction code from the app/ package,
create a complete feature table for all windows,
understand why training and deployment must use the same feature extraction logic.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.2 Setup

from pathlib import Path
import sys

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

current_path = Path.cwd().resolve()

for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents):
    if (parent / "app").exists():
        PROJECT_ROOT = parent
        break
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.")

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from app.config import WINDOW_SIZE
from app.data_loader import load_wisdm_phone_accel
from app.windowing import create_windows
from app.feature_extraction import extract_features
from app.pipeline import add_magnitude, windows_to_features

DATA_DIR = PROJECT_ROOT / "data"

df = load_wisdm_phone_accel(DATA_DIR)
df = add_magnitude(df)







ImportantSoftware Engineering Principle



The feature extraction logic lives in app/feature_extraction.py.
The workbook imports and uses this function instead of redefining it.
This ensures that training and live prediction use the same implementation.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.3 Why Feature Engineering?
A sensor window contains many raw measurements.
For example, with a window size of 100 samples and four signals (x, y, z, magnitude), one window contains 400 raw values.
Classical machine learning models such as Random Forests usually work better with structured tabular features.
Feature engineering transforms a window into a compact numerical summary.





flowchart LR

A[Sensor Window]
-->B[Feature Extraction]

B-->C[Feature Vector]

C-->D[Machine Learning Model]

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.4 Our Feature Set
For each signal
x, y, z, magnitude
we compute:



Feature
Meaning




mean
average signal level


std
variability


min
smallest value


max
largest value


energy
overall signal intensity



This gives
\[
4 \times 5 = 20
\]
features per window.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.5 Step 1 — Create One Example Window

walking_df = (
    df[df["activity"] == "walking"]
    .sort_values("timestamp")
    .copy()
)

windows = create_windows(
    walking_df,
    window_size=WINDOW_SIZE,
)

example_window = windows[0]

example_window.head()

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.7 Step 3 — Understand the Energy Feature
Energy is computed as
\[
Energy = \sum_i x_i^2
\]
It is often useful because dynamic activities such as walking usually produce higher signal intensity than static activities such as sitting or standing.

example_window["magnitude"].head()

303992     8.292063
303993     8.807811
303994     9.902309
303995    11.387479

energy_magnitude = np.sum(example_window["magnitude"] ** 2)

energy_magnitude

At this point, the continuous sensor data has been transformed into a tabular machine learning dataset.
Each row represents one window.
Each column represents one feature or metadata field.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.10 Step 6 — Save the Feature Table

features_df.to_csv(
    DATA_DIR / "features_df.csv",
    index=False,
)







NoteRemember



Saving intermediate artifacts makes the pipeline easier to debug and reproduce.
The next chapters can load the feature table directly instead of recomputing it every time.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.11 Think
Which feature do you expect to be most useful for distinguishing walking from standing?

mean?
standard deviation?
maximum?
energy?

Why?

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.12 Common Pitfall






WarningTraining and Deployment Must Match



If you train a model using one feature extraction method but deploy it with a different method, predictions become unreliable.
That is why the project keeps feature extraction in app/feature_extraction.py.

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.13 Checkpoint
Before continuing, you should be able to answer:

Why do we extract features from windows?
Why does a Random Forest work well with feature tables?
What does the energy feature measure?
How many features do we compute per window?
Why must training and deployment use the same feature extraction logic?

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:

median

title: 6  Feature Engineering
text:
6.15 Summary
In this chapter you learned

why feature engineering is necessary,
how windows are transformed into feature vectors,
how reusable project code improves maintainability,
how to create a complete feature table,
why consistent feature extraction is essential for deployment.

In the next chapter we will train a machine learning model using this feature table.

title: 7  Machine Learning
text:
7.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to

title: 7  Machine Learning
text:
load the feature table created in the previous chapter,
separate model features from labels and subject identifiers,
train a Random Forest baseline model,
inspect predictions and class probabilities,
understand why reusable training code matters.

title: 7  Machine Learning
text:
7.2 Setup

from pathlib import Path
import sys

import pandas as pd

current_path = Path.cwd().resolve()

for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents):
    if (parent / "app").exists():
        PROJECT_ROOT = parent
        break
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.")

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from app.training import (
    load_feature_table,
    split_features_and_labels,
    train_random_forest,
)

title: 7  Machine Learning
text:
7.3 Why Random Forest?
We use a Random Forest as the first model because it is a strong baseline for tabular feature data.
A Random Forest is an ensemble of decision trees.
It is useful because it is

easy to train,
robust,
fast enough for this project,
suitable for tabular data,
relatively easy to explain.







TipEngineering Insight



A simple baseline model is often the best starting point.
Before using deep learning, always ask whether a simpler model already solves the problem well enough.

title: 7  Machine Learning
section: 7.5 Step 2 — Split Features, Labels and Subjects
text:
7.5 Step 2 — Split Features, Labels and Subjects
The model should learn from numerical features.
It should not use metadata such as subject_id as input.

X, y, groups, feature_columns = split_features_and_labels(features_df)

X.shape, y.shape, groups.shape

The exact feature column order must be saved with the model.
During deployment, live features must be passed to the model in the same order.

title: 7  Machine Learning
text:
7.6 Step 3 — Train the Baseline Model
We now train a Random Forest on the complete feature table.
This gives us a baseline model artifact that later chapters can evaluate and export with metadata.

baseline_model = train_random_forest(X, y)

baseline_model

RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook. On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.RandomForestClassifier?Documentation for RandomForestClassifieriFitted
        
            
                Parameters
                









n_estimators  n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22.
200



random_state  random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary <random_state>` for details.
42



criterion  criterion: {"gini", "entropy", "log_loss"}, default="gini"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria are
"gini" for the Gini impurity and "log_loss" and "entropy" both for the
Shannon information gain, see :ref:`tree_mathematical_formulation`.
Note: This parameter is tree-specific.
'gini'



max_depth  max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples.
None



min_samples_split  min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions.
2



min_samples_leaf  min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions.
1



min_weight_fraction_leaf  min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided.
0.0



max_features  max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default="sqrt"

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to `"sqrt"`.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features.
'sqrt'



max_leaf_nodes  max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes.
None



min_impurity_decrease  min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19
0.0



bootstrap  bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree.
True



oob_score  oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`.
False



n_jobs  n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
<n_jobs>` for more details.
None



verbose  verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting.
0



warm_start  warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary <warm_start>` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details.
False



class_weight  class_weight: {"balanced", "balanced_subsample"}, dict or list of dicts, default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given, all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems, a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example,
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 5}, {0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 1}] instead of
[{1:1}, {2:5}, {3:1}, {4:1}].

The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

The "balanced_subsample" mode is the same as "balanced" except that
weights are computed based on the bootstrap sample for every tree
grown.

For multi-output, the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified.
None



ccp_alpha  ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22
0.0



max_samples  max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples irrespective of
`sample_weight`.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max_samples * X.shape[0]` unweighted samples
or `max_samples * sample_weight.sum()` weighted samples.

.. versionadded:: 0.22

.. versionchanged:: 1.9
Float `max_samples` is relative to `sample_weight.sum()` instead of
`X.shape[0]` for weighted samples.
None



monotonic_cst  monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonic increase
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonic decrease

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`),
- multioutput classifications (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`).

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide <monotonic_cst_gbdt>`.

.. versionadded:: 1.4
None




            
        
    
        
            
                Fitted attributes
                








Name
Type
Value


classes_  classes_: ndarray of shape (n_classes,) or a list of such arrays

The classes labels (single output problem), or a list of arrays of
class labels (multi-output problem).
ndarray[object](3,)
['sitting','standing','walking']


estimator_  estimator_: :class:`~sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeClassifier`

The child estimator template used to create the collection of fitted
sub-estimators.

.. versionadded:: 1.2
`base_estimator_` was renamed to `estimator_`.
DecisionTreeClassifier
DecisionTreeClassifier()


estimators_  estimators_: list of DecisionTreeClassifier

The collection of fitted sub-estimators.
list
[DecisionTreeC...te=1608637542), DecisionTreeC...te=1273642419), DecisionTreeC...te=1935803228), DecisionTreeC...ate=787846414), ...]


estimators_samples_  estimators_samples_: list of arrays

The subset of drawn samples (i.e., the in-bag samples) for each base
estimator. Each subset is defined by an array of the indices selected.

.. versionadded:: 1.4
list
[array([2523, ..., dtype=int32), array([4782, ..., dtype=int32), array([5462, ..., dtype=int32), array([2848, ..., dtype=int32), ...]


feature_importances_  feature_importances_: ndarray of shape (n_features,)

The impurity-based feature importances.
The higher, the more important the feature.
The importance of a feature is computed as the (normalized)
total reduction of the criterion brought by that feature. It is also
known as the Gini importance.

Warning: impurity-based feature importances can be misleading for
high cardinality features (many unique values). See
:func:`sklearn.inspection.permutation_importance` as an alternative.
ndarray[float64](20,)
[0.02,0.08,0.02,...,0.06,0.08,0.04]


feature_names_in_  feature_names_in_: ndarray of shape (`n_features_in_`,)

Names of features seen during :term:`fit`. Defined only when `X`
has feature names that are all strings.

.. versionadded:: 1.0
ndarray[object](20,)
['mean_x','std_x','min_x',...,'min_magnitude','max_magnitude', 'energy_magnitude']


n_classes_  n_classes_: int or list

The number of classes (single output problem), or a list containing the
number of classes for each output (multi-output problem).
int
3


n_features_in_  n_features_in_: int

Number of features seen during :term:`fit`.

.. versionadded:: 0.24
int
20


n_outputs_  n_outputs_: int

The number of outputs when ``fit`` is performed.
int
1




            
        
    



baseline_model.classes_

array(['sitting', 'standing', 'walking'], dtype=object)

title: 7  Machine Learning
text:
7.10 Summary
In this chapter you learned how to

load the saved feature table,
split features, labels and subject identifiers,
train a reusable Random Forest baseline,
inspect one prediction and its probabilities.

In the next chapter we will evaluate whether the model generalizes to people it has never seen before.

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
explain why random train/test splits can be optimistic,
evaluate a model with a subject-wise split,
understand the leave-one-subject-out evaluation idea,
compare random split and subject-wise results,
export model metadata with evaluation results.

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.2 Setup

from pathlib import Path
import sys

import pandas as pd

current_path = Path.cwd().resolve()

for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents):
    if (parent / "app").exists():
        PROJECT_ROOT = parent
        break
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.")

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from app.config import WINDOW_SIZE
from app.evaluation import (
    confusion_matrix_table,
    evaluate_random_split,
    evaluate_subject_split,
    leave_one_subject_out_evaluation,
    metrics_for_metadata,
)
from app.training import (
    load_feature_table,
    save_model_artifacts,
    split_features_and_labels,
    train_random_forest,
)


features_df = load_feature_table()
X, y, groups, feature_columns = split_features_and_labels(features_df)

features_df.shape

(8066, 22)

title: 8  Evaluation
section: 8.3 Why Random Split Can Be Optimistic
text:
8.3 Why Random Split Can Be Optimistic
A random split divides windows into training and test rows.
This is easy to do, but it can mix windows from the same person across both sets.
For wearable data, this matters because people move differently.






WarningCommon Pitfall



If windows from the same subject appear in both training and test sets, the model may partly learn person-specific movement patterns.
The test score can then look better than performance on a completely new person.

text:

Can the model classify unseen windows from a similar pool of subjects?

That is useful, but it is not the same as testing on a new person.

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.5 Step 2 — Subject-Wise Evaluation
In a subject-wise split, entire subjects are assigned to either training or test.
The model is evaluated on people it did not see during training.

subject_result = evaluate_subject_split(X, y, groups)

This score answers the more realistic deployment question:

This is usually harder.
Sitting and standing are especially difficult because both can produce low-motion signals.

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.6 Step 3 — Compare Strategies

comparison = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "strategy": "random_split",
            "accuracy": random_result["metrics"]["accuracy"],
            "test_size": random_result["test_size"],
        },
        {
            "strategy": "subject_split",
            "accuracy": subject_result["metrics"]["accuracy"],
            "test_size": subject_result["test_size"],
        },
    ]
)

comparison

subject_split
0.867876
1544








8.6.1 Discussion
If the random split score is higher than the subject-wise score, that is not a bug.
It is evidence that generalization to new people is a harder problem than recognizing windows from familiar subjects.

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.7 Step 4 — LOSO Concept
Leave-one-subject-out evaluation trains one model per subject.
Each run holds out exactly one subject for testing and trains on all remaining subjects.
This gives a more detailed view of which subjects are easy or hard to generalize to.
For a teaching render, we run a small preview on the first five subjects.

loso_preview = leave_one_subject_out_evaluation(
    X,
    y,
    groups,
    max_subjects=5,
)

loso_preview

mean     0.985082
std      0.020436
min      0.961905
25%      0.963504
50%      1.000000
75%      1.000000
max      1.000000
Name: accuracy, dtype: float64








NoteRemember



A full LOSO evaluation is useful, but it trains one model per subject.
For this dataset, that means 51 model fits.

title: 8  Evaluation
section: 8.8 Step 5 — Export Model Artifacts and Metadata
text:
8.8 Step 5 — Export Model Artifacts and Metadata
We train one final model on all available feature rows and save the artifacts needed later:

trained model,
feature column order,
class labels,
model metadata,
evaluation results.


final_model = train_random_forest(X, y)

metadata = {
    "evaluation_results": {
        "random_split": metrics_for_metadata(random_result),
        "subject_split": metrics_for_metadata(subject_result),
        "loso_preview": loso_preview.to_dict(orient="records"),
    }
}

model_metadata = save_model_artifacts(
    final_model,
    feature_columns,
    metadata=metadata,
    window_size=WINDOW_SIZE,
)

model_metadata.keys()

dict_keys(['model_type', 'window_size', 'feature_columns', 'class_labels', 'n_estimators', 'random_state', 'evaluation_results'])



{
    "model_type": model_metadata["model_type"],
    "window_size": model_metadata["window_size"],
    "class_labels": model_metadata["class_labels"],
}

{'model_type': 'RandomForestClassifier',
 'window_size': 100,
 'class_labels': ['sitting', 'standing', 'walking']}

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.9 Checkpoint
Before continuing, you should be able to answer:

Why can a random split be optimistic?
What changes in a subject-wise split?
What does LOSO evaluate?
Why is generalization to new people harder?
Why do we save feature columns with the model?

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.10 Further Exploration
Optional: run LOSO for all subjects by removing max_subjects=5.
How much do subject-level accuracies vary?
Which activities are most often confused?

title: 8  Evaluation
text:
8.11 Summary
In this chapter you learned how to

compare random split and subject-wise evaluation,
interpret why subject-wise evaluation is more realistic,
use a LOSO preview,
export model artifacts with metadata.

The next chapters will later turn these artifacts into a service and live demo.

title: 9  Deployment
text: This chapter turns the trained model into a reusable prediction service.

title: 10  Live Prediction
text: This chapter connects phyphox, Streamlit, and the trained model for live activity recognition.
